claims.flip_evidence_text: provided that no event, circumstance, development, change, occurrence or effect to the extent resulting from, arising out of, or relating to any of the following shall be deemed to constitute, or shall be taken into account in determining whether there has been, a Company Material Adverse Effect, or whether a Company Material Adverse Effect would reasonably be expected to occur: (ii) any changes after the date hereof in conditions generally affecting the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages
claims.notes: Clause (ii) generally excludes industry changes, but the claim omits the material exception that applies to clauses (i), (ii), (vii), and (ix): such changes are NOT excluded to the extent they have a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company relative to others in the industry.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Company Material Adverse Effect” means any event, circumstance, development, change, occurrence or effect that, individually or in the aggregate, has had or would reasonably be expected to have, a material adverse effect on the financial condition, assets, liabilities, business or results of operations of the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole; provided that no event, circumstance, development, change, occurrence or effect to the extent resulting from, arising out of, or relating to any of the following shall be deemed to constitute, or shall be taken into account in determining whether there has been, a Company Material Adverse Effect, or whether a Company Material Adverse Effect would reasonably be expected to occur: (i) any changes after the date hereof in general United States or global economic conditions, including changes in United States or global securities, credit, financial, debt or other capital markets, (ii) any changes after the date hereof in conditions generally affecting the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages, (iii) any reduction in the assets under management of the Company or any of its Subsidiaries (it being understood and agreed that this clause (iii) shall not preclude Parent from asserting that any facts or occurrences giving rise to or contributing to such reduction that are not otherwise excluded from the definition of Company Material Adverse Effect should be deemed to constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been, or would reasonably be expected to be, a Company Material Adverse Effect), (iv) any decline, in and of itself, in the market price or trading volume of the Company Non-Voting Common Stock, any changes in credit ratings and any changes in any analysts’ recommendations or ratings with respect to the Company or any of its Subsidiaries (it being understood and agreed that this clause (iv) shall not preclude Parent from asserting that any facts or occurrences giving rise to or contributing to such decline that are not otherwise excluded from the definition of Company Material Adverse Effect should be deemed to constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been, or would reasonably be expected to be, a Company Material Adverse Effect), (v) any failure, in and of itself, by the Company or any of its Subsidiaries to meet any internal or published projections, forecasts, estimates or predictions in respect of revenues, earnings or other financial or operating metrics for any period (it being understood and agreed that this clause (v) shall not preclude Parent from asserting that any facts or occurrences giving rise to or contributing to such failure that are not otherwise excluded from the definition of Company Material Adverse Effect should be deemed to constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been, or would reasonably be expected to be, a Company Material Adverse Effect), (vi) the execution and delivery of this Agreement, the public announcement or the pendency of this Agreement (it being understood and agreed that this clause (vi) shall not apply with respect to any representation or warranty that is intended to address the consequences of the execution and delivery of this Agreement or the public announcement or the pendency of this Agreement), (vii) any changes after the date hereof in any Applicable Law or GAAP (or authoritative interpretations thereof), (viii) any action or omission taken by the Company pursuant to the written request of Parent or the Merger Subs or (ix) any acts of God, natural disasters, terrorism, armed hostilities, sabotage, war or any escalation or worsening of acts of war, epidemic, pandemic or disease outbreak (including the COVID-19 pandemic), except in the case of each of clauses (i), (ii), (vii) or (ix), to the extent that any such event, circumstance, development, change, occurrence or effect has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to the adverse effect such event, circumstance, development, change, occurrence or effect has on other companies operating in the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages; it being agreed, for purposes of this Agreement, that the COVID-19 pandemic has not, as of the 7 date of this Agreement, had such a materially disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole. (Pages 12-13)

Question:
Changes in Target's industry (Y/N)

Llama answer:
Yes, changes in Target's industry are excluded from constituting a Company Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (ii) of the definition.

Canonical claims:
- claim-ad7495651c98: Yes, changes in Target's industry are excluded from constituting a Company Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (ii) of the definition.

messages.content:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Company Material Adverse Effect” means any event, circumstance, development, change, occurrence or effect that, individually or in the aggregate, has had or would reasonably be expected to have, a material adverse effect on the financial condition, assets, liabilities, business or results of operations of the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole; provided that no event, circumstance, whether there has been, a Company Material Adverse Effect, or whether a Company Material Adverse Effect would reasonably be expected to occur: (i) any changes after the date hereof in general United Agreement), (vii) any changes after the date hereof in any Applicable Law or GAAP (or authoritative interpretations thereof), (viii) any action or omission taken by the Company pursuant to the written request of Parent or the Merger Subs or (ix) any acts of God, natural disasters, terrorism, armed hostilities, sabotage, war or any escalation or worsening of acts of war, epidemic, pandemic or has on other companies operating in the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages; it being agreed, for purposes of this Agreement, that the COVID-19 pandemic has not, as of the 7 date of this Agreement, had such a materially disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole. (Pages 12-13)

Question:
Changes in Target's industry (Y/N)

Llama answer:
Yes, changes in Target's industry are excluded from constituting a Company Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (ii) of the definition.

Canonical claims:
- claim-ad7495651c98: Yes, changes in Target's industry are excluded from constituting a Company Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (ii) of the definition.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator. I need to evaluate the canonical claim against the contract excerpt, question, and model answer.

Claim: "Yes, changes in Target's industry are excluded from constituting a Company Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (ii) of the definition."

Let me analyze the contract excerpt:
- Clause (ii) says: "any changes after the date hereof in conditions generally affecting the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages"
- This is listed as something that shall not be deemed to constitute or be taken into account in determining CMAE.
- However, there's an exception at the end: "except in the case of each of clauses (i), (ii), (vii) or (ix), to the extent that any such event... has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company... relative to... other companies operating in the industries..."

So changes in industry conditions are generally excluded, UNLESS they have a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company relative to others in the industry.

The claim says "changes in Target's industry are excluded" without mentioning the disproportionate effect exception. Is this fully true? The claim is a broad statement that they are excluded, but the exclusion is not absolute - there's a carve-out for disproportionate effects.

Wait, let me look at the exact wording of the claim again: "Yes, changes in Target's industry are excluded from constituting a Company Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (ii) of the definition."

This is technically partially true because while clause (ii) does exclude them generally, it doesn't exclude them if there's a disproportionate effect. However, looking at the model answer (Llama answer), it says the same thing. The question is "Changes in Target's industry (Y/N)" which seems to be asking whether changes in the target's industry are excluded.

Actually, let me reconsider. The claim states they are excluded "as per clause (ii)". Clause (ii) does provide the exclusion, subject to the disproportionate effect exception. Is the claim false because it omits the exception? Or is it partially true?

In legal annotation tasks like this, usually:
- If a claim states a general rule but omits a material exception, it's often "partially_true" or "false" depending on whether the exception swallows the rule or is just a carve-out.
- Here, the exception is significant: if the effect is disproportionate, it IS taken into account.

However, looking at the Llama answer, it matches the claim exactly. The question seems to be a simple Y/N question where the expected answer might be "Yes" because clause (ii) is the relevant provision.

But I need to be careful. The claim says "are excluded" which is a categorical statement. Since they are NOT excluded if disproportionate, the claim is not fully true. It is partially true because clause (ii) does exclude them generally.

Wait, I should check if "Target" refers to "Company" here. Yes, in M&A contracts, Target = Company.

Let me think about the labels:
- correctness_label: partially_true (because it ignores the disproportionate impact exception)
- load_bearing_label: Is there evidence text that if flipped would change the answer? The claim cites clause (ii). If clause (ii) didn't exist, the answer would be different. So yes, clause (ii) is load bearing. But wait - the exception is also load bearing. However, the claim specifically cites clause (ii) as the basis. The evidence text for clause (ii) is load bearing. But the exception is in the same paragraph. 

Actually, looking at the contract, the exception applies to clauses (i), (ii), (vii), and (ix). So for clause (ii), the exclusion is subject to the disproportionate effect test.

If the claim is "Yes, changes in Target's industry are excluded... as per clause (ii)", the load bearing text would be the text of clause (ii) itself. But also the exception text is load bearing because if we flip the exception, the claim would be fully true rather than partially true? No, the exception limits the exclusion.

Let me reconsider the "load_bearing" definition. Usually, load bearing means: if you changed this evidence, would the claim's truth value change? Or is it the evidence that supports the claim?

Actually, in these annotation schemes, "load_bearing_label: yes" means the claim relies on specific evidence text from the contract. The "flip_evidence_text" is the text that if flipped/removed would change the claim's support.

For this claim, the load bearing text is clause (ii): "any changes after the date hereof in conditions generally affecting the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages"

But wait - the claim is partially true because of the disproportionate exception. The exception text is: "except in the case of each of clauses (i), (ii), (vii) or (ix), to the extent that any such event, circumstance, development, change, occurrence or effect has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to the adverse effect such event, circumstance, development, change, occurrence or effect has on other companies operating in the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages"

If the claim ignores this exception, is the claim partially true? Yes.

But let me look at similar annotation guidelines. Often:
- If the claim captures the main point but misses a nuance/exception, it's "partially_true"
- If the claim is fully supported by the text without material omission, it's "true"

Is the disproportionate effect exception material? Yes, it's a significant carve-out. So "partially_true" seems right.

However, I need to consider whether the question format implies a simpler answer. The question is "Changes in Target's industry (Y/N)". This looks like a checklist question where the answer should be "Yes" (meaning they are excluded). The Llama answer says "Yes...". The claim restates the Llama answer.

In many contract QA datasets, the "canonical claims" are meant to be evaluated against the contract. If the claim is an accurate but incomplete summary, it might be "partially_true".

Let me think about "load_bearing". The claim cites clause (ii). The text of clause (ii) is definitely load bearing. The flip_evidence_text should be the specific text that supports the claim. But if the claim is only partially true, is the evidence still load bearing? Yes, the claim does rely on clause (ii) for its partial truth.

Actually, I need to be more careful. The instruction says: "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". This suggests I need to provide the text that is the evidence for the claim. The "flip" might mean "evidence that could be flipped to change the label" or it might just mean "the evidence text". Given the name "flip_evidence_text", it likely means the specific contract text that the claim is based on / that if contradicted would flip the claim.

Let me provide:
- correctness_label: partially_true (because the exclusion in clause (ii) is subject to the disproportionate effect exception)
- load_bearing_label: yes (because the claim explicitly relies on clause (ii))
- flip_evidence_text: the text of clause (ii) and potentially the intro text. Actually, I should provide the specific evidence text that supports the claim. The claim says "as per clause (ii)". So the evidence is: "(ii) any changes after the date hereof in conditions generally affecting the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages"

But wait, the claim says changes are excluded. The exclusion is established by the introductory language: "no event... to the extent resulting from... any of the following shall be deemed to constitute... a Company Material Adverse Effect". So the full evidence includes both the intro and clause (ii).

However, the exception text is also relevant because it qualifies clause (ii). Since the claim omits it, the claim is partially true.

Let me reconsider if it could be "true". In the context of the question "Changes in Target's industry (Y/N)", the answer "Yes" (they are excluded) might be considered correct at a high level, with the disproportionate effect being a secondary nuance. But legally, exceptions are material. "Changes in Target's industry are excluded" is simply false as an absolute statement because they are NOT excluded if disproportionate.

Actually, looking at the exact claim wording: "Yes, changes in Target's industry are excluded from constituting a Company Material Adverse Effect, as per clause (ii) of the definition."

This is a claim that: (1) changes in Target's industry are excluded, and (2) this is per clause (ii).
Clause (ii) does say that, subject to the exception. So the claim is partially true.

I think partially_true is the safest and most accurate label.

For load_bearing: yes, because the claim explicitly references clause (ii) and depends on it.

For flip_evidence_text: I should quote the relevant text. I'll include the operative exclusion language and clause (ii).

Let me draft:
correctness_label: partially_true
load_bearing_label: yes
flip_evidence_text: provided that no event, circumstance, development, change, occurrence or effect to the extent resulting from, arising out of, or relating to any of the following shall be deemed to constitute, or shall be taken into account in determining whether there has been, a Company Material Adverse Effect, or whether a Company Material Adverse Effect would reasonably be expected to occur: (ii) any changes after the date hereof in conditions generally affecting the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages
notes: Omits the disproportionate-adverse-effect exception that applies to clause (ii).

Wait, I should check if the exception is material enough to make it partially_true vs false. If someone says "X is excluded" and the contract says "X is excluded except when Y", then saying "X is excluded" without qualification is misleading but captures the general rule. "Partially_true" captures that it is true in part (the general exclusion exists) but false in part (the exception is ignored).

Could it be false? If I say "Changes in Target's industry are excluded" and the contract says they are excluded UNLESS disproportionate, then strictly speaking they are not categorically excluded. However, the claim does say "as per clause (ii)", which is the provision that establishes the exclusion (with the exception). I think partially_true is better than false because clause (ii) does indeed provide the exclusion.

Let me check if there's any other issue. The claim says "Target's industry" while the contract says "industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages". "Target" = "Company" in this context, so that's fine.

One more thing: the question is "Changes in Target's industry (Y/N)". The Llama answer is "Yes...". The claim is essentially the same as the Llama answer. If the task is to evaluate whether the claim is a correct interpretation of the contract, then partially_true is appropriate.

Actually, looking at the instruction again: "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." This suggests I should compare the claim to the model answer as well? Or just use them as context? The model answer (Llama answer) is identical to the claim. But I should evaluate the claim against the contract.

I think my analysis holds:
- partially_true
- yes
- evidence text
- note about exception

Let me refine the evidence text. The claim specifically cites clause (ii). The most load-bearing text is clause (ii) itself, but the exclusionary language is also necessary. I'll provide a concise but complete quote.

Actually, looking at the format, I need to return JSON or a structured output for each claim. The user said "For each claim return:". There's only one claim: claim-ad7495651c98.

I'll format it clearly.

One final check: Is the disproportionate exception part of clause (ii)? No, it's a general exception at the end of the list that applies to clauses (i), (ii), (vii), and (ix). So clause (ii) standing alone does exclude industry changes. The exception is a separate sentence. So "as per clause (ii)" is accurate for the exclusion, but the overall definition includes the exception. This reinforces partially_true.

Final decision:
correctness_label: partially_true
load_bearing_label: yes
flip_evidence_text: "(ii) any changes after the date hereof in conditions generally affecting the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages" (plus perhaps the intro). I'll include the key phrase from clause (ii) and the intro language that says these shall not be deemed to constitute a CMAE.
notes: The exclusion in clause (ii) is subject to the disproportionate-adverse-effect exception for clauses (i), (ii), (vii), and (ix).

Let me make sure the evidence text is exact from the contract. I'll quote:
"provided that no event, circumstance, development, change, occurrence or effect to the extent resulting from, arising out of, or relating to any of the following shall be deemed to constitute, or shall be taken into account in determining whether there has been, a Company Material Adverse Effect, or whether a Company Material Adverse Effect would reasonably be expected to occur: (ii) any changes after the date hereof in conditions generally affecting the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries materially engages"

That's a bit long but accurate. Alternatively, just the clause (ii) text since the claim cites it specifically. But the exclusionary effect comes from the "provided that no event..." intro. I'll include both for completeness.

Actually, looking at the name "flip_evidence_text", it might be asking for the text that if flipped (i.e., if the opposite were true) would change the label. For example, if clause (ii) said industry changes ARE included, then the claim would be false. So the text of clause (ii) is the flip evidence. I'll provide the clause (ii) text and the surrounding operative language.

I'll go with this.
